summary: Research on the Nakdong River Basin demonstrated that sewage and wastewater treatment plant effluents significantly elevated algal growth potential (AGP), with the highest values recorded in the Kumho River (42.8%) and the middle Nakdong River (22.7%) when algal samples were mixed with 20% effluent. Elevated phosphorus and ammonium concentrations in the effluents, alongside higher total nitrogen, phosphorus, and conductivity, were identified as key drivers of eutrophic conditions. The study employed luminescent bacterial toxicity tests to assess effluent safety, linking nutrient enrichment directly to increased algal proliferation. This work underscores the critical role of point-source nutrient pollution from wastewater in stimulating algal blooms within a major South Korean river system.
